flashcards.back: A linear data structure where elements (called nodes) are stored in non-contiguous memory locations. Each node contains data and a pointer/reference to the next node, forming a chain-like structure.

flashcards.front: What are the components of a node in a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: Each node has two parts: DATA (stores the actual element) and NEXT POINTER (stores address of the next node). The last node's next pointer points to NULL.

flashcards.front: What are the three main types of linked lists?
flashcards.back: SINGLY LINKED LIST (unidirectional - only forward traversal), DOUBLY LINKED LIST (bidirectional - can traverse forward and backward), and CIRCULAR LINKED LIST (last node points back to first node).

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of linked lists over arrays?
flashcards.back: DYNAMIC SIZE (can grow/shrink at runtime), NO MEMORY WASTAGE (no pre-allocation), EFFICIENT INSERTION/DELETION (no shifting elements like arrays), and FLEXIBLE MEMORY ALLOCATION.

flashcards.front: What are the disadvantages of linked lists compared to arrays?
flashcards.back: HIGHER MEMORY USAGE (each node needs extra pointer space), NO RANDOM ACCESS (must traverse from head), MORE COMPLEX CODE (manual pointer manipulation), and NO CACHE LOCALITY (scattered memory locations).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of basic operations in a linked list?
flashcards.back: INSERTION at beginning: O(1), INSERTION at end: O(n), DELETION at beginning: O(1), DELETION at end: O(n), SEARCH: O(n), ACCESS by index: O(n).

flashcards.front: What is a header node in a linked list?
flashcards.back: A header node is a dummy node placed at the beginning of the linked list that contains no actual data but simplifies insertion and deletion operations at the beginning of the list.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between singly and doubly linked lists?
flashcards.back: SINGLY: each node has one pointer (next), can traverse only forward. DOUBLY: each node has two pointers (prev and next), can traverse both forward and backward, enabling easier deletion and reverse traversal.

flashcards.front: How do you insert a node at the beginning of a linked list?
flashcards.back: Create new node, set its next pointer to current head, then update head to point to new node. This operation takes O(1) time.

flashcards.front: How do you delete a node from the end of a singly linked list?
flashcards.back: Traverse to second-last node, set its next pointer to NULL, free the memory of last node. Requires O(n) time as traversal is needed.

flashcards.front: What is a circular linked list?
flashcards.back: A variation where the last node's next pointer points back to the first node (head) instead of NULL, creating a circle. Can be singly or doubly circular.

flashcards.front: What is the NULL pointer in linked lists used for?
flashcards.back: NULL indicates the end of the linked list. It marks that no next node exists, used as a sentinel value to stop traversal and identify the last node.
Traversal is the process of visiting each node in the linked list exactly once, typically starting from the head node and following next pointers until NULL is reached.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between static and dynamic memory allocation in linked lists?
flashcards.back: Static allocation uses fixed-size arrays with compile-time memory. Dynamic allocation (using malloc in C, new in C++, or in Python/Java) allocates memory at runtime as needed.
